=======================================================
  COMPLETE ASSIGNMENT GUIDE
  NBA Game Outcome Prediction — ML Assignment
=======================================================

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
  PART 1: SETUP & RUNNING THE CODE
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

STEP 1 — Install Python & Jupyter
──────────────────────────────────
  1. Download Anaconda: https://www.anaconda.com/products/distribution
     (Anaconda installs Python + Jupyter + most ML libraries at once)
  2. OR install manually:
       pip install jupyter notebook

STEP 2 — Install Required Libraries
──────────────────────────────────────
  Open terminal / Anaconda Prompt and run:

    pip install pandas numpy matplotlib seaborn scikit-learn jupyter

STEP 3 — Download the Dataset
───────────────────────────────
  1. Go to: https://www.kaggle.com/datasets/nathanlauga/nba-games
  2. Click "Download" (you need a free Kaggle account)
  3. Extract the zip — you need the file called: games.csv
  4. Place games.csv in the SAME folder as all 3 notebooks

STEP 4 — Run the Notebooks
────────────────────────────
  1. Open terminal in your project folder
  2. Run: jupyter notebook
  3. Your browser will open Jupyter
  4. Open each notebook and click:
       Kernel → Restart & Run All
  5. Wait for all cells to complete
  6. Note down all metric values (Accuracy, F1, AUC etc.)
  7. Fill these values into the results table in the PDF report

STEP 5 — Update the Report
────────────────────────────
  After running all 3 notebooks, fill in the comparison table
  in Section 4 of the report with your actual results.
  Re-generate the PDF (or edit it in Word if needed).

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
  PART 2: GITHUB SETUP & COMMIT STRATEGY
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

STEP 1 — Create GitHub Repository
────────────────────────────────────
  1. Go to https://github.com and sign in (or create a free account)
  2. Click "New Repository"
  3. Name it: ml-nba-prediction
  4. Set visibility: PUBLIC (required for submission)
  5. Check "Add README"
  6. Click "Create Repository"

STEP 2 — Install Git & Clone Repo
────────────────────────────────────
  Download Git: https://git-scm.com/downloads
  Then in terminal:

    git clone https://github.com/YOUR_USERNAME/ml-nba-prediction
    cd ml-nba-prediction

STEP 3 — Add Your Files
─────────────────────────
  Copy these files into your cloned repo folder:
    - notebook1_random_forest.ipynb
    - notebook2_svm.ipynb
    - notebook3_knn.ipynb
    - games.csv  (or add Kaggle link to README)
    - members.txt
    - submission.txt
    - ML_Assignment_Report.pdf

STEP 4 — Commit Strategy (IMPORTANT for marks!)
─────────────────────────────────────────────────
  The assignment requires "detailed commit history."
  Use these commits — each member should push their own:

  COMMIT 1 (Member 1):
    git add .
    git commit -m "Initial project setup and README"
    git push origin main

  COMMIT 2 (Member 1):
    git add notebook1_random_forest.ipynb
    git commit -m "Add Notebook 1: Random Forest - data loading and preprocessing"
    git push origin main

  COMMIT 3 (Member 1):
    git add notebook1_random_forest.ipynb
    git commit -m "Add Notebook 1: Random Forest - model training and GridSearchCV"
    git push origin main

  COMMIT 4 (Member 1):
    git add notebook1_random_forest.ipynb
    git commit -m "Add Notebook 1: Random Forest - evaluation metrics, confusion matrix, ROC curve"
    git push origin main

  COMMIT 5 (Member 2):
    git add notebook2_svm.ipynb
    git commit -m "Add Notebook 2: SVM - data preprocessing and correlation analysis"
    git push origin main

  COMMIT 6 (Member 2):
    git add notebook2_svm.ipynb
    git commit -m "Add Notebook 2: SVM - model training with RBF kernel and GridSearchCV"
    git push origin main

  COMMIT 7 (Member 2):
    git add notebook2_svm.ipynb
    git commit -m "Add Notebook 2: SVM - evaluation, kernel comparison, ROC curve"
    git push origin main

  COMMIT 8 (Member 3):
    git add notebook3_knn.ipynb
    git commit -m "Add Notebook 3: KNN - feature distributions and preprocessing"
    git push origin main

  COMMIT 9 (Member 3):
    git add notebook3_knn.ipynb
    git commit -m "Add Notebook 3: KNN - optimal K selection and model training"
    git push origin main

  COMMIT 10 (Member 3):
    git add notebook3_knn.ipynb
    git commit -m "Add Notebook 3: KNN - evaluation, distance metric comparison, ROC curve"
    git push origin main

  COMMIT 11 (Any member):
    git add ML_Assignment_Report.pdf members.txt submission.txt
    git commit -m "Add final report, members.txt, and submission.txt"
    git push origin main

  COMMIT 12 (Any member after updating results):
    git add .
    git commit -m "Update report with actual model performance results and comparison table"
    git push origin main

STEP 5 — README.md Template
──────────────────────────────
  Edit your README.md on GitHub with this content:

  # NBA Game Outcome Prediction — ML Assignment
  
  Predicting NBA game outcomes using supervised machine learning.
  
  ## Algorithms Used
  - Random Forest Classifier (Notebook 1)
  - Support Vector Machine (Notebook 2)
  - K-Nearest Neighbors (Notebook 3)
  
  ## Dataset
  NBA Games Dataset — https://www.kaggle.com/datasets/nathanlauga/nba-games
  
  ## Setup
  pip install pandas numpy matplotlib seaborn scikit-learn jupyter
  
  Place games.csv in the root folder, then run each notebook.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
  PART 3: VIDEO PRESENTATION GUIDE
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

Tools to record:
  - OBS Studio (free): https://obsproject.com
  - Zoom (record your screen share)
  - Loom (easy screen recorder): https://loom.com

Structure (max 20 mins total, max 4 mins per person):

  MEMBER 1 (4 mins):
    - Introduce the project: problem, dataset
    - Show the dataset (games.csv), explain features
    - Run/show Notebook 1 (Random Forest)
    - Share results: accuracy, confusion matrix, feature importance

  MEMBER 2 (4 mins):
    - Explain SVM conceptually (hyperplane, kernel trick)
    - Run/show Notebook 2 (SVM)
    - Show kernel comparison chart
    - Share results

  MEMBER 3 (4 mins):
    - Explain KNN conceptually (distance, neighbors)
    - Run/show Notebook 3 (KNN)
    - Show K selection graph
    - Share results

  ANYONE (4 mins):
    - Show the comparison table (all 3 models side by side)
    - Discuss which model performed best and why
    - Mention limitations and future work

Upload to YouTube as "Unlisted" and paste the link into submission.txt.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
  PART 4: VIVA PREPARATION (Q&A GUIDE)
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

General Questions Every Member Should Know:
────────────────────────────────────────────

Q: What is supervised learning?
A: A type of ML where the model is trained on labeled data (input + known output).
   The model learns to map inputs to outputs. Examples: classification, regression.

Q: Why did you choose this dataset?
A: It's real-world, publicly available on Kaggle, has clear features and a
   binary target, and sports analytics is a practical ML application domain.

Q: What is a confusion matrix?
A: A 2x2 table showing True Positives, False Positives, True Negatives, False Negatives.
   It helps evaluate beyond just accuracy — especially useful for imbalanced classes.

Q: What is the difference between precision and recall?
A: Precision = of all predicted positives, how many were actually positive.
   Recall = of all actual positives, how many did we correctly predict.
   F1-score is the harmonic mean of both.

Q: What is ROC-AUC?
A: ROC curve plots True Positive Rate vs False Positive Rate at different thresholds.
   AUC = Area Under Curve. AUC of 1.0 = perfect; 0.5 = random guessing.

Q: Why did you use StandardScaler?
A: SVM and KNN are distance-based algorithms. Without scaling, features with
   larger ranges (e.g., AST = 20-40) dominate over smaller ranges (FG_PCT = 0.4-0.6).
   StandardScaler makes all features have mean=0 and std=1.

Q: What is cross-validation?
A: A technique to assess generalization. The training data is split into K folds;
   the model is trained on K-1 folds and validated on 1 fold, K times. The average
   score is reported. We used 5-fold CV.

Q: What is overfitting?
A: When the model performs well on training data but poorly on unseen test data.
   It memorizes noise instead of learning patterns.

─────────────────────────────────────────────
Random Forest Specific Questions (Member 1):
─────────────────────────────────────────────

Q: How does Random Forest work?
A: It builds many decision trees, each trained on a random subset of data and
   features (bagging + feature randomness). For classification, it takes the
   majority vote across all trees.

Q: What is n_estimators?
A: The number of decision trees in the forest. More trees = more robust but slower.

Q: What is max_depth?
A: The maximum depth of each tree. Limiting depth prevents overfitting.

Q: What does feature importance show?
A: How much each feature contributes to reducing impurity (Gini) across all trees.
   Higher importance = feature has more influence on predictions.

Q: Why is Random Forest better than a single Decision Tree?
A: Random Forest reduces variance by averaging many trees, making it more robust
   and less prone to overfitting.

─────────────────────────────────────────────
SVM Specific Questions (Member 2):
─────────────────────────────────────────────

Q: How does SVM work?
A: It finds the hyperplane that maximizes the margin between the two classes.
   Support vectors are the data points closest to the hyperplane.

Q: What is the kernel trick?
A: When data is not linearly separable, the kernel function maps data into a
   higher-dimensional space where a linear hyperplane can separate them.
   Common kernels: RBF (Gaussian), Linear, Polynomial.

Q: What is the C parameter in SVM?
A: Regularization parameter. Small C = wider margin, more misclassifications allowed.
   Large C = smaller margin, tries to classify all training points correctly (risk of overfitting).

Q: What is the RBF kernel?
A: Radial Basis Function kernel. It maps data into infinite-dimensional space.
   Controlled by gamma: low gamma = smooth boundary, high gamma = complex boundary.

Q: Why is SVM slow on large datasets?
A: SVM's training complexity is O(n^2) to O(n^3) in the number of samples,
   making it slow for datasets with tens of thousands of rows.

─────────────────────────────────────────────
KNN Specific Questions (Member 3):
─────────────────────────────────────────────

Q: How does KNN work?
A: For a new data point, it finds the K closest training points (by distance)
   and assigns the majority class among those K neighbors.

Q: How did you choose K?
A: We tested K from 1 to 30, plotted test accuracy vs K, and selected the K
   that maximized test accuracy (the elbow point).

Q: What distance metric does KNN use?
A: Default is Euclidean distance. We also compared Manhattan and Chebyshev.
   Euclidean: sqrt(sum of squared differences)
   Manhattan: sum of absolute differences
   Chebyshev: maximum absolute difference across dimensions

Q: What is the bias-variance tradeoff in KNN?
A: Low K (e.g., K=1) = low bias, high variance (overfits to training data).
   High K = high bias, low variance (underfits, too smooth boundary).

Q: What are KNN's disadvantages?
A: 1. Slow prediction: must compare to all training points.
   2. Sensitive to irrelevant features and feature scale.
   3. Does not build an explicit model (lazy learner).

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
  PART 5: FINAL SUBMISSION CHECKLIST
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

Before zipping your ML-assignment.zip, confirm:

  [ ] members.txt — contains all 3 members' IDs, emails
  [ ] submission.txt — dataset link + GitHub link + YouTube link
  [ ] notebook1_random_forest.ipynb — all cells run with output visible
  [ ] notebook2_svm.ipynb — all cells run with output visible
  [ ] notebook3_knn.ipynb — all cells run with output visible
  [ ] ML_Assignment_Report.pdf — includes source code as Appendix (text, not screenshots)
  [ ] GitHub repo is PUBLIC with at least 10 meaningful commits
  [ ] YouTube video is uploaded (Unlisted or Public), max 20 mins
  [ ] Zip file is named exactly: ML-assignment.zip
  [ ] One team member uploads the zip to the courseweb

  TURNITIN WARNING: Keep similarity below 20%.
  Write the discussion, introduction and conclusion sections in your own words.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
